narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.80 and a middle-class family background, you've clearly put in strong effort to build a promising profile for law school. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your probability of failure at 59.7%—just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This decision was driven primarily by your school decile rankings from Year 1 and Year 3, both at the 1st decile, which stood out as the strongest factors pushing the prediction toward failure compared to typical students who average around the 5th or 6th decile. Your undergraduate GPA, while well above the dataset average of 3.21, acted as a significant counterbalance, strongly pulling the prediction in the direction of passing the bar. Your Year 1 decile ranking had a notable but lesser influence toward the failure prediction, while your LSAT score of 28—below the typical 36.7—nudged it slightly in that direction as well. Being a full-time student, which is the norm for over 92% of applicants, and your middle-income bracket had minimal impact overall. In essence, while your GPA shines brighter than most, your lower school deciles in later years outweighed it for this model, unlike many successful applicants who maintain higher consistent rankings. We understand this is disappointing, especially given your strengths. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the average and reflecting on what might have affected your Year 3 performance—perhaps targeted academic support could help elevate future deciles. Strengthening these areas could shift future predictions in your favor for other programs. You're already ahead with that impressive GPA, so keep building on it. We wish you the best in your next steps toward law school.
